title.emphasis: Section 2: Navigating Employment Classification: The AB 5, AB 2257, and Borello Framework

section.para: The most significant legal hurdle for any entity engaging an individual for services in California is the state's stringent worker classification law. Assembly Bill 5 (AB 5), and its subsequent clarifying legislation, AB 2257, established a new paradigm that presumes all workers are employees. Understanding how to navigate this framework is the central strategic challenge for creating a minimalist and legally compliant patron-artist model. The goal is not to circumvent the law, but to understand and meet the specific criteria the law provides for a legitimate independent contractor relationship.

title.emphasis: Subsection 2.1: The Default Presumption: Understanding the Strict ABC Test

section.para: Effective January 1, 2020, AB 5 codified the California Supreme Court's decision in Dynamex Operations West, Inc. v. Superior Court, establishing the "ABC test" as the default standard for determining worker classification for most purposes under the California Labor Code and Unemployment Insurance Code.23 This test begins with the strong legal presumption that a worker is an employee.23
To rebut this presumption and classify a worker as an independent contractor, the hiring entity bears the burden of proving that all three of the following conditions are met 23:

listitem.para: (A) The worker is free from the control and direction of the hiring entity in connection with the performance of the work, both under the contract and in fact.

para.emphasis: (B) The worker performs work that is outside the usual course of the hiring entity’s business.

listitem.para: (C) The worker is customarily engaged in an independently established trade, occupation, or business of the same nature as the work performed.

section.para: The failure to satisfy even one of these prongs results in the worker being classified as an employee by default.25 For many patrons or arts organizations, prong (B) presents an insurmountable obstacle. If a patron's "business" is collecting art, or an arts nonprofit's mission is to foster new art, then the work performed by a commissioned artist could be construed as being within the "usual course" of that business, causing the test to fail. This strict, all-or-nothing standard makes it imperative to find a statutory exemption.

title.emphasis: Subsection 2.2: The "Fine Artist" Exemption: The Primary Pathway to Independent Contractor Status

section.para: In response to concerns from numerous industries, including the arts, the legislature passed AB 2257, which amended AB 5 and expanded the list of occupations and professional services that are exempt from the ABC test. For these exempt categories, worker classification is instead determined by the more flexible, multi-factor test established in S.G. Borello & Sons, Inc. v. Department of Industrial Relations (the "Borello test").2
Among the "professional services" exemptions is a specific carve-out for a "fine artist". AB 2257 provides a statutory definition for this term, clarifying who may qualify for the exemption:

A "fine artist" is an individual who creates works of art to be appreciated primarily or solely for their imaginative, aesthetic, or intellectual content, including drawings, paintings, sculptures, mosaics, works of calligraphy, works of graphic art, crafts, or mixed media.
This definition is broad enough to cover the majority of artists engaged in typical commission scenarios, making it the primary legal pathway for the proposed model.

title.emphasis: Subsection 2.3: Meeting the Bare Minimum: The Six Conditions for the Exemption

section.para: Crucially, the "fine artist" exemption is not automatic. To qualify for the exemption and have the relationship evaluated under the Borello test, the hiring entity must demonstrate that the artist meets a series of six specific conditions. These conditions represent the "bare minimum" requirements that the artist must satisfy to operate as a legitimate independent business in the eyes of the state.

row.entry: Requirement
Actionable Step for the Artist
1\. **Maintains a business location separate from the hiring entity.**
The artist's home studio, even if in a parent's house, satisfies this requirement. The location must be distinct from the patron's location.
2\. **Has a business license, in addition to any required professional licenses or permits.**
The artist must apply for and obtain a business license if one is required by their local jurisdiction (e.g., the City of Chico). This is a non-negotiable prerequisite.
3\. **Has the ability to set or negotiate their own rates for the services performed.**
The artist must have the freedom to determine their own pricing structure, which should be reflected in the SOW for each commission.
4\. **Has the ability to set their own hours, outside of project completion dates and reasonable business hours.**
The artist must control their own work schedule, free from a mandated 9-to-5-style requirement from the patron.
5\. **Is customarily engaged in an independently established business of the same nature as the work performed.**
The artist must hold themselves out to the public as being in the business of creating art. This can be demonstrated through a portfolio, website, or by contracting with other clients.
6\. **Customarily and regularly exercises discretion and independent judgment in the performance of the services.**
The artist must have creative control over their work, consistent with the nature of being a skilled professional, rather than simply following rote instructions.

section.para: The user's initial premise of engaging an artist without a business license is fundamentally incompatible with the legal structure that provides the desired flexibility. The business license is not a superfluous piece of bureaucracy to be sidestepped; it is the statutory key that unlocks the "fine artist" exemption. Without satisfying this and the other five conditions, the relationship defaults back to the strict ABC test, which it would almost certainly fail, resulting in worker misclassification and significant legal liability for the patron. Therefore, the "bare minimum" for this model is not to do nothing, but to do precisely what the exemption statute demands.

title.emphasis: Subsection 2.4: Application of the Borello Test: A Multi-Factor Analysis

section.para: Successfully meeting the six conditions for the "fine artist" exemption does not automatically classify the artist as an independent contractor. Instead, it earns the patron and artist the right to have their relationship evaluated under the older, more holistic Borello standard.2

Unlike the rigid, pass/fail nature of the ABC test, the Borello test is a multi-factor "economic realities" test that weighs numerous considerations to determine the true nature of the working relationship.27 No single factor is controlling; rather, a court or agency looks at the totality of the circumstances.24 The most significant factor is whether the hiring entity has the
right to control the manner and means of accomplishing the desired result, not just the result itself.29
Other key factors considered under Borello include:

listitem.para: Whether the worker is engaged in a distinct occupation or business.
Whether the worker or the hiring entity supplies the instrumentalities, tools, and the place of work.
The worker's investment in the equipment or materials required for their task.
The special skill required in the occupation.
The worker's opportunity for profit or loss depending on their managerial skill.
The degree of permanence of the working relationship.
The method of payment (by time or by the job).
The belief of the parties regarding the nature of the relationship.

section.para: The strategic goal of the entire model is to structure the relationship in such a way that it clearly satisfies the six prerequisites for the exemption and then overwhelmingly weighs in favor of independent contractor status under the Borello factors.

row.entry: **ABC Test (The Default)**
***Borello*** **Test (The Exemption Goal)**
**Strict & Rigid:** Hiring entity must prove **ALL THREE** prongs. Failing one means the worker is an employee.
**Flexible & Holistic:** A multi-factor test weighing the "totality of the circumstances." No single factor is determinative.
**Prong A:** Worker is free from control and direction.
**Primary Factor:** Right of the hiring entity to control the "manner and means" of the work.
**Prong B:** Work is outside the usual course of the hiring entity's business.
**Secondary Factors Include:**
**Prong C:** Worker is customarily engaged in an independently established business.
\- Worker engaged in a distinct business.
\- Who supplies tools and workplace.
\- Worker's investment in their business.
\- Skill required.
\- Opportunity for profit or loss.
\- Permanence of the relationship.
\- Method of payment (job vs. time).
\- Belief of the parties.
